1:
"Wherefore when we could no longer forbear, we thought it good to be left at Athens alone;" The brief verse notes Paul's willingness to be left alone at Athens for their sake. The grammatical voices note the strain of anxiety. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's account of his concern: "when we could no longer forbear (endure, contain our anxiety, hold out)" — his longing and worry for the Thessalonians grew so great that he could bear the suspense no longer.

On "we thought it good to be left at Athens alone," the witnesses note the sacrifice. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's decision: "we thought it good (resolved) to be left at Athens alone" — he chose to send Timothy to them (v.2), even though it meant being left by himself at Athens, deprived of his companion's help, in a strange and hostile city.

On "alone," the puritan Henry notes the cost: to be "alone" at Athens, without his fellow-workers, was a real sacrifice; Paul's love for the Thessalonians made him willing to bear solitude for their comfort. The verse displays Paul's willingness to be left alone at Athens for their sake.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's anxiety — "when we could no longer forbear (contain our concern)," his worry growing past bearing (Barnes, Robertson); "we thought it good to be left at Athens alone," resolving to send Timothy even at the cost of his own solitude (Barnes, Calvin); to be "alone" at Athens a real sacrifice, borne for their comfort (Henry). The verse displays the willing sacrifice. The verse marks the anxiety: could no longer forbear. The verse displays the decision: left at Athens. The verse displays the solitude: alone. The verse displays the love: for their sake. The decision displays Paul sacrificing his comfort for them. The verse displays the forbearing: ended. The verse displays the Athens: alone. The verse displays the cost: solitude. Paul's willingness to be left alone at Athens for their sake displays the strength of his concern: unable to bear his anxiety for the Thessalonians any longer, he resolved to send Timothy to them — choosing to remain by himself at Athens, deprived of his companion, for their comfort. The verse displays the apostle's anxious love, sending Timothy to establish them (v.2).
2:
"And sent Timotheus, our brother, and minister of God, and our fellowlabourer in the gospel of Christ, to establish you, and to comfort you concerning your faith:" The witnesses note Timothy sent to establish and comfort them in their faith. On the threefold commendation, the grammatical voices note Timothy's standing. Barnes and Calvin note the honour Paul gives him: "Timotheus, our brother, and minister of God (a servant of God in the gospel), and our fellowlabourer in the gospel of Christ" — three titles marking Timothy's worth, that the Thessalonians might receive him as a weighty messenger.

On "to establish you," the witnesses note the first aim. Barnes and Robertson note the purpose: "to establish you (sterixai — to make firm, strengthen, confirm)" — Timothy was sent to steady and strengthen the Thessalonians, that their faith might not be shaken by persecution.

On "to comfort you concerning your faith," the Reformed Calvin notes the second aim: "and to comfort (encourage, exhort) you concerning your faith" — to encourage them in their faith amid their trials; the double purpose was to confirm and to console. The verse displays Timothy sent to establish and comfort them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the threefold commendation — "Timotheus, our brother, minister of God, and fellowlabourer in the gospel of Christ," that they receive him as a weighty messenger (Barnes, Calvin); "to establish you (make firm, strengthen)," steadying them against persecution (Barnes, Robertson); "to comfort you concerning your faith," encouraging them amid trials (Calvin). The verse displays Timothy's mission. The verse marks the messenger: Timothy. The verse displays the titles: brother, minister, fellowlabourer. The verse displays the first aim: establish you. The verse displays the second: comfort you. The mission displays Timothy sent to strengthen and console. The verse displays the Timothy: commended. The verse displays the establishing: of faith. The verse displays the comfort: in faith. Timothy sent to establish and comfort them in their faith displays the care behind Paul's sacrifice: he sent Timothy — his brother, God's minister, and fellow-labourer — to strengthen and confirm the Thessalonians and to encourage them in their faith, that their persecutions might not shake them. The verse displays the strengthening mission, guarding them against being moved (v.3).
3:
"That no man should be moved by these afflictions: for yourselves know that we are appointed thereunto." The brief verse notes that believers are appointed to afflictions and should not be moved. The grammatical voices note the steadying aim. Barnes and Robertson note the purpose of Timothy's mission: "that no man should be moved (saine — shaken, disturbed, swayed, as a dog wagging or as one beguiled) by these afflictions" — that none of them be unsettled or turned aside from the faith by the persecutions they endured.

On "these afflictions," the witnesses note the trials. Barnes and Calvin note that "these afflictions" were the persecutions the Thessalonians were then suffering — real and pressing trials that might tempt the weak to abandon the faith.

On "yourselves know that we are appointed thereunto," the Reformed Calvin notes the divine appointment: "yourselves know that we (Christians) are appointed (set, destined) thereunto" — afflictions are no strange accident but the appointed lot of believers; suffering is part of the Christian calling, foretold and ordained, so it should not surprise or shake them. The verse displays that believers are appointed to afflictions.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the steadying aim — "that no man should be moved (shaken, swayed) by these afflictions," none unsettled by persecution (Barnes, Robertson); "these afflictions" the present persecutions, pressing trials (Barnes, Calvin); "yourselves know that we are appointed (destined) thereunto," affliction the appointed lot of believers, part of the calling, not to surprise or shake them (Calvin). The verse displays appointed afflictions. The verse marks the aim: not be moved. The verse displays the trials: these afflictions. The verse displays the appointment: appointed thereunto. The verse displays the calling: suffering ordained. The teaching displays believers steadied by knowing affliction is their lot. The verse displays the moving: prevented. The verse displays the affliction: appointed. The verse displays the knowledge: forewarned. That believers are appointed to afflictions and should not be moved displays the steadying truth Paul taught: the Thessalonians were not to be shaken by their persecutions, for they knew that affliction is the appointed lot of Christians — ordained, foretold, and part of the calling. The verse displays the appointed sufferings of the faithful, as Paul had forewarned them (v.4).
4:
"For verily, when we were with you, we told you before that we should suffer tribulation; even as it came to pass, and ye know." The brief verse notes Paul's forewarning of the tribulation that came to pass. The grammatical voices note the prediction. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul had prepared them in advance: "when we were with you, we told you before (forewarned you) that we should suffer tribulation" — during his ministry among them, Paul had plainly predicted that suffering would come.

On "even as it came to pass," the witnesses note the fulfilment. Barnes and Ellicott note that the prediction was confirmed by the event: "even as it came to pass (so it happened), and ye know" — the persecution Paul foretold had now actually occurred, exactly as he said; the fulfilment proved he had told them the truth.

The grammatical-historical note the purpose: Paul's forewarning, now fulfilled, was meant to steady them — since the suffering was predicted and expected, it should not unsettle their faith but confirm it. The verse displays Paul's forewarning of the tribulation that came to pass.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul had prepared them — "when we were with you, we told you before (forewarned) that we should suffer tribulation," plainly predicting suffering (Barnes, Robertson); "even as it came to pass, and ye know," the prediction confirmed by the event, exactly as foretold (Barnes, Ellicott); the forewarning meant to steady them, the fulfilment confirming faith (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fulfilled forewarning. The verse marks the prediction: told you before. The verse displays the content: suffer tribulation. The verse displays the fulfilment: came to pass. The verse displays the purpose: to steady them. The forewarning displays Paul's prediction confirmed by the event. The verse displays the telling: beforehand. The verse displays the tribulation: foretold. The verse displays the fulfilment: exact. Paul's forewarning of the tribulation that came to pass displays the steadying force of his foresight: he had plainly told the Thessalonians, while with them, that suffering would come — and it had now happened exactly as foretold, so that the very fulfilment, far from shaking them, confirmed the truth of his word. The verse displays the forewarned suffering, prompting Paul's anxious inquiry (v.5).
5:
"For this cause, when I could no longer forbear, I sent to know your faith, lest by some means the tempter have tempted you, and our labour be in vain." The witnesses note Paul's fear lest the tempter had undone his labour. On "when I could no longer forbear, I sent to know your faith," the grammatical voices note the renewed anxiety. Barnes and Robertson note Paul repeats his anxious concern: "when I could no longer forbear (contain my anxiety), I sent (Timothy) to know your faith" — to learn whether their faith stood firm under persecution.

On "lest by some means the tempter have tempted you," the witnesses note the fear. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul's specific dread: "lest by some means the tempter (ho peirazon — Satan, the one who tempts) have tempted you" — he feared that Satan, using their afflictions, might have seduced them from the faith; persecution is the tempter's tool.

On "and our labour be in vain," the Reformed Calvin notes the further fear: "and our labour be in vain (fruitless, lost)" — if they fell, all Paul's toil among them would be wasted; his anxiety was for the fruit of his ministry as well as for their souls. The verse displays Paul's fear lest the tempter had undone his labour.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's renewed anxiety — "when I could no longer forbear, I sent to know your faith," to learn if it stood firm (Barnes, Robertson); his dread "lest by some means the tempter (Satan) have tempted you," fearing Satan used their afflictions to seduce them (Barnes, Chrysostom); "and our labour be in vain," lest all his toil be wasted if they fell (Calvin). The verse displays his anxious fear. The verse marks the inquiry: to know your faith. The verse displays the dread: the tempter. The verse displays the danger: tempted from the faith. The verse displays the fear: labour in vain. The fear displays Paul anxious lest Satan undo his work. The verse displays the faith: tested. The verse displays the tempter: feared. The verse displays the labour: at risk. Paul's fear lest the tempter had undone his labour displays the depth of his pastoral anxiety: unable to bear the suspense, he sent to learn whether their faith held — dreading that Satan might have used their afflictions to seduce them, and that all his toil among them would be lost. The verse displays the apostle's anxious care, relieved by Timothy's good report (vv.6-8).
6:
"But now when Timotheus came from you unto us, and brought us good tidings of your faith and charity, and that ye have good remembrance of us always, desiring greatly to see us, as we also to see you:" The witnesses note Timothy's good tidings of their faith, love, and longing for Paul. On "Timotheus came from you unto us, and brought us good tidings of your faith and charity," the grammatical voices note the welcome report. Barnes and Robertson note the relief of Timothy's return: he "brought us good tidings (euangelisamenou — brought good news, the word used of the gospel itself) of your faith and charity (love)" — the report that their faith and love stood firm was itself like good news.

On "that ye have good remembrance of us always," the witnesses note their affection. Barnes and Calvin note that the Thessalonians "have good remembrance of us always" — they remembered Paul with affection and gratitude, not turned against him by his absence or by his enemies' slanders.

On "desiring greatly to see us, as we also to see you," the puritan Henry notes the mutual longing: "desiring greatly to see us, as we also to see you" — the longing was mutual; as Paul yearned for them, so they yearned for him; love answered love. The verse displays Timothy's good tidings of their faith, love, and longing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the welcome report — Timothy "brought us good tidings (good news, as of the gospel) of your faith and charity (love)," their faith and love standing firm (Barnes, Robertson); "good remembrance of us always," remembering Paul with affection, not turned against him (Barnes, Calvin); "desiring greatly to see us, as we also to see you," the longing mutual, love answering love (Henry). The verse displays the good tidings. The verse marks the report: faith and charity. The verse displays the affection: good remembrance. The verse displays the longing: mutual desire. The verse displays the relief: good news. The tidings display the Thessalonians firm in faith and love. The verse displays the faith: standing. The verse displays the love: firm. The verse displays the longing: mutual. Timothy's good tidings of their faith, love, and longing for Paul display the relief that ended his anxiety: Timothy returned with news, welcome as the gospel itself, that the Thessalonians stood firm in faith and love, remembered Paul with affection, and longed to see him as he longed for them. The verse displays the comforting report, which revived the apostle (vv.7-8).
7:
"Therefore, brethren, we were comforted over you in all our affliction and distress by your faith:" The brief verse notes Paul comforted in his own distress by their faith. The grammatical voices note the comfort. Barnes and Robertson note the effect of Timothy's report on Paul: "we were comforted (parakalethemen — encouraged, consoled) over you... by your faith" — the news of the Thessalonians' steadfast faith brought the anxious apostle real consolation.

On "in all our affliction and distress," the witnesses note Paul's own trials. Barnes and Calvin note that this comfort came "in all our affliction (thlipsis — pressure, trouble) and distress (anagke — necessity, hardship)" — Paul himself was then in the midst of his own sufferings (at Corinth), yet the good news of their faith consoled him even there.

On "by your faith," the grammatical-historical note the source: it was specifically "by your faith" — their steadfastness in believing, despite persecution, that comforted him; their faith was his consolation. The verse displays Paul comforted in his own distress by their faith.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the effect of the report — "we were comforted (encouraged, consoled) over you by your faith," the news of their steadfastness consoling the anxious apostle (Barnes, Robertson); this comfort coming "in all our affliction (pressure) and distress (hardship)," Paul amid his own sufferings yet consoled (Barnes, Calvin); "by your faith," their steadfast believing his consolation (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the comfort. The verse marks the comfort: over you. The verse displays the occasion: affliction and distress. The verse displays the source: your faith. The verse displays the relief: consolation. The comfort displays Paul consoled amid his trials by their faith. The verse displays the comfort: received. The verse displays the affliction: Paul's own. The verse displays the faith: theirs. Paul comforted in his own distress by their faith displays the power of good news of the saints: amid all his own affliction and hardship, the report of the Thessalonians' steadfast faith brought the anxious apostle real consolation — their faith was his comfort in his trouble. The verse displays the apostle's consolation, so that he is revived (v.8).
8:
"For now we live, if ye stand fast in the Lord." The brief verse notes that Paul lives when they stand fast in the Lord. The grammatical voices note the strong expression. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's vivid words: "for now we live, if ye stand fast (steko — stand firm, hold your ground) in the Lord" — their steadfastness was so dear to him that he speaks of it as his very life; their standing firm was, to him, like living.

On "now we live," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Calvin note the hyperbole of love: Paul's anxiety had been like death to him; now, hearing they stood firm, he "lives" — revived, restored, his burden lifted; their welfare was bound up with his own happiness.

On "if ye stand fast in the Lord," the Reformed Calvin notes the condition: his joy depends on their perseverance — "if ye stand fast in the Lord" — their continued steadfastness in union with Christ. The verse displays that Paul lives when they stand fast.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's vivid words — "now we live, if ye stand fast (stand firm) in the Lord," their steadfastness so dear it is like his very life (Barnes, Robertson); the hyperbole of love, his anxiety like death, their firmness reviving him (Barnes, Calvin); his joy depending on their perseverance "in the Lord," their continued steadfastness (Calvin). The verse displays his revived life. The verse marks the life: now we live. The verse displays the condition: if ye stand fast. The verse displays the figure: revived by their firmness. The verse displays the bond: their welfare his life. The expression displays Paul living in their steadfastness. The verse displays the living: revived. The verse displays the standing: fast. The verse displays the Lord: their ground. That Paul lives when they stand fast in the Lord displays the depth of his bond with them: their steadfastness was so precious to him that he counts it his very life — hearing they stood firm in the Lord, the burden of his anxiety lifted and he was revived. The verse displays the apostle's joy in their standing, overflowing in thanksgiving (vv.9-10).
9:
"For what thanks can we render to God again for you, for all the joy wherewith we joy for your sakes before our God;" The brief verse notes Paul's overflowing thanksgiving for the joy they gave him. The grammatical voices note the question of gratitude. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's overflowing thanks: "what thanks can we render to God again for you?" — his gratitude is so great that he asks what adequate return of thanks he can possibly make to God for them; words fail his thankfulness.

On "for all the joy wherewith we joy for your sakes," the witnesses note the abounding joy. Barnes and Calvin note the cause: "all the joy wherewith we joy for your sakes" — the great gladness their faith and steadfastness brought him; his joy in them was the measure of his thankfulness.

On "before our God," the grammatical-historical note the setting: this joy is "before our God" — in God's presence, a holy joy offered up to God, not a merely natural gladness; Paul rejoices over them as in the sight of God. The verse displays Paul's overflowing thanksgiving for the joy they gave him.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's overflowing thanks — "what thanks can we render to God again for you?", his gratitude so great that words fail (Barnes, Robertson); "all the joy wherewith we joy for your sakes," the gladness their faith brought him, the measure of his thanks (Barnes, Calvin); "before our God," a holy joy offered in God's presence (grammatical-historical). The verse displays his thanksgiving. The verse marks the question: what thanks can we render. The verse displays the cause: all the joy. The verse displays the source: for your sakes. The verse displays the setting: before our God. The thanksgiving displays Paul overflowing with grateful joy. The verse displays the thanks: overflowing. The verse displays the joy: abounding. The verse displays the God: before him. Paul's overflowing thanksgiving for the joy they gave him displays the abundance of his grateful gladness: so great is the joy their steadfast faith brings him that he asks what adequate thanks he can render to God for them — a holy joy rejoiced before God himself. The verse displays the apostle's grateful joy, pouring out in prayer to see them (v.10).
10:
"Night and day praying exceedingly that we might see your face, and might perfect that which is lacking in your faith?" The brief verse notes Paul's earnest prayer to see them and perfect their faith. The grammatical voices note the intensity of prayer. Barnes and Robertson note the fervour: "night and day praying exceedingly (hyperekperissou — beyond all measure, super-abundantly)" — Paul prayed with the utmost earnestness, constantly and intensely, for the Thessalonians.

On "that we might see your face," the witnesses note the first request. Barnes and Calvin note his longing: "that we might see your face" — to be reunited with them in person, the desire that had run through the whole chapter.

On "and might perfect that which is lacking in your faith," the Reformed Calvin notes the second request: "and might perfect (katartisai — complete, mend, supply, put in order) that which is lacking (the deficiencies) in your faith" — though their faith was strong, it was not yet complete; Paul longed to come and supply what was still wanting, to build them up further. The verse displays Paul's earnest prayer to see them and perfect their faith.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fervour — "night and day praying exceedingly (beyond all measure)," with utmost earnestness (Barnes, Robertson); "that we might see your face," longing to be reunited (Barnes, Calvin); "and might perfect (complete, supply) that which is lacking in your faith," to build up their still-incomplete faith (Calvin). The verse displays the earnest prayer. The verse marks the intensity: night and day exceedingly. The verse displays the first request: see your face. The verse displays the second: perfect their faith. The verse displays the aim: supply what is lacking. The prayer displays Paul interceding to come and build them up. The verse displays the praying: exceeding. The verse displays the seeing: their face. The verse displays the perfecting: of faith. Paul's earnest prayer to see them and perfect their faith displays the goal of his longing: he prayed night and day, beyond all measure, that he might be reunited with the Thessalonians and supply what was still lacking in their faith — to build them up to completeness. The verse displays the apostle's intercession, leading into his prayer for their increase (vv.11-13).
11:
"Now God himself and our Father, and our Lord Jesus Christ, direct our way unto you." The witnesses note Paul's prayer that God and Christ direct his way to them. On "God himself and our Father, and our Lord Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the joint address. Barnes and Robertson note the prayer addressed to both: "God himself and our Father, and our Lord Jesus Christ" — Father and Son named together as the one to whom Paul prays; and notably the verb that follows is singular, the Father and the Son addressed as one.

On the joint address, the witnesses note the Christology. Barnes and Calvin note the significance: Paul prays to the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together, with a singular verb ("direct") — a witness to the unity of the Son with the Father, both addressed as the one God who governs his way.

On "direct our way unto you," the grammatical-historical note the petition: "direct (kateuthynai — make straight, guide, clear the road of obstacles) our way unto you" — Paul asks God to remove the hindrances (Satan had blocked him, 2:18) and make a straight path for his return. The verse displays Paul's prayer that God and Christ direct his way to them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prayer addressed to both — "God himself and our Father, and our Lord Jesus Christ," Father and Son named together, with a singular verb following (Barnes, Robertson); the joint address with the singular "direct" a witness to the Son's unity with the Father, both the one God who governs (Barnes, Calvin); "direct (make straight, clear of obstacles) our way unto you," asking God to remove the hindrances and make a straight path (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prayer for his way. The verse marks the address: Father and Son. The verse displays the unity: singular verb. The verse displays the petition: direct our way. The verse displays the aim: remove obstacles. The prayer displays Paul asking God to clear his path to them. The verse displays the God: addressed. The verse displays the Christ: with him. The verse displays the way: directed. Paul's prayer that God and Christ direct his way to them displays his dependence and his Christology: he prays to the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together — with a singular verb that joins them as one — that God would clear away the hindrances and make a straight path for his longed-for return. The verse displays the prayer for guidance, joined to a prayer for their growth in love (v.12).
12:
"And the Lord make you to increase and abound in love one toward another, and toward all men, even as we do toward you:" The brief verse notes Paul's prayer that their love increase toward all. The grammatical voices note the petition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's prayer for their growth in love: "the Lord make you to increase and abound (pleonasai kai perisseusai — multiply and overflow) in love" — that their love might not merely continue but grow and superabound.

On "one toward another, and toward all men," the witnesses note the scope. Barnes and Calvin note the double reach: love "one toward another (within the church, mutual brotherly love) and toward all men (even outsiders, all mankind)" — a love that begins among the brethren but extends to everyone, not confined to their own circle.

On "even as we do toward you," the grammatical-historical note the pattern: their love is to grow "even as we (Paul and his companions) do toward you" — Paul's own overflowing love for them is the measure and model of the love he prays they may have. The verse displays Paul's prayer that their love increase toward all.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's prayer for growth in love — "the Lord make you to increase and abound (multiply and overflow) in love," that it grow and superabound (Barnes, Robertson); the double reach "one toward another (in the church) and toward all men (even outsiders)," love beginning among the brethren but extending to all (Barnes, Calvin); "even as we do toward you," Paul's own love the measure and model (grammatical-historical). The verse displays abounding love. The verse marks the prayer: increase and abound. The verse displays the grace: love. The verse displays the scope: one another and all men. The verse displays the pattern: Paul's love. The prayer displays love growing toward all, modelled on Paul's. The verse displays the increase: prayed for. The verse displays the love: overflowing. The verse displays the all: included. Paul's prayer that their love increase toward all displays the growth he longs to see: that the Lord would make the Thessalonians multiply and overflow in love — both within the church and toward all mankind — after the very pattern of Paul's own abounding love for them. The verse displays the prayer for love, aimed at unblameable holiness (v.13).
13:
"To the end he may stablish your hearts unblameable in holiness before God, even our Father, at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ with all his saints." The witnesses note the prayer for unblameable holiness at Christ's coming — with the question of "all his saints." On "stablish your hearts unblameable in holiness," the grammatical voices note the aim. Barnes and Robertson note the goal of their abounding love: "to the end he may stablish (sterixai — make firm, confirm) your hearts unblameable (without fault) in holiness before God" — that love may issue in a settled, blameless holiness; abounding love is the means, established holiness the end.

On "before God, even our Father, at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the day. Barnes and Ellicott note that the hearts are to be unblameable "before God... at the coming (parousia) of our Lord Jesus Christ" — holiness to stand the scrutiny not only through life but in the day of judgment, when Christ returns.

On "with all his saints," interpreters divide. Barnes takes "all his saints (hagioi — holy ones)" to include both the holy angels who come with him (Matthew 25:31) and all the redeemed. Ellicott leans rather to the glorified believers — anticipating 4:14, and noting these "saints" seem to be assessors in the judgment, an honour for holy men — while allowing the word might be stretched to include the angels (Deuteronomy 33:2; Jude 14). The verse displays the prayer for unblameable holiness at Christ's coming.

The witnesses agree the prayer: that abounding love may establish their hearts unblameable in holiness before God, fit to stand at the coming of the Lord Jesus (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). But "with all his saints" divides them: Barnes takes it of both the holy angels (who attend Christ, Matthew 25:31) and all the redeemed; Ellicott leans to the glorified believers (anticipating 4:14, as assessors in the judgment), while allowing it might stretch to the angels (Deuteronomy 33:2; Jude 14). The verse displays the holiness crux. The verse marks the aim: unblameable in holiness. The verse displays the setting: before God. The verse displays the day: Christ's coming. The verse displays the debate: saints as angels or glorified men. The prayer displays love issuing in blameless holiness at the parousia. The verse displays the hearts: established. The verse displays the holiness: unblameable. The verse displays the saints: attending. The prayer for unblameable holiness at Christ's coming, with all his saints, displays the end of Paul's intercession and a crux: that their abounding love may establish them blameless in holiness, fit to stand before God at Christ's coming — attended by "all his saints," understood as the holy angels and the redeemed (Barnes) or chiefly the glorified believers (Ellicott). The verse displays the goal of blameless holiness, closing the chapter and opening the exhortations to sanctification (ch.4).
